draft.introduction: Luke 15 contains three parables of the lost—the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son. Together they form Jesus’ most sustained defense of his association with sinners and tax collectors.

translation.text: Now the tax collectors and sinners were all drawing near to hear him. And the Pharisees murmured, ‘This man receives sinners and eats with them.’ So he told them this parable: ‘What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine and go after the one that is lost?’
translation.original_text: ἦσαν δὲ αὐτῷ ἐγγίζοντες πάντες οἱ τελῶναι καὶ οἱ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἀκούειν αὐτοῦ

translation.text: And he said, ‘There was a man who had two sons. And the younger said, Father, give me the share of property that is coming to me. But when he came to himself, he arose and went to his father. And his father saw him and had compassion, and ran and embraced him.’
translation.original_text: εἶπεν δέ · ἄνθρωπός τις εἶχεν δύο υἱούς

translator_notes.note: The father running to meet the son (δραμὼν) would have been considered undignified for a patriarch in the ancient Near East. This detail emphasizes the radical nature of divine mercy in Jesus’ teaching.
